key_points: Quadrilateral: a polygon with 4 sides and 4 angles; sum of interior angles = 360°
Types include: square, rectangle, parallelogram, rhombus, trapezium, kite, general quadrilateral
Diagonals: join opposite vertices; can bisect each other, be equal, or perpendicular depending on type
Parallelogram properties: opposite sides parallel & equal, opposite angles equal, diagonals bisect each other
Rectangle: all properties of parallelogram + diagonals equal
Rhombus: all sides equal + diagonals bisect at right angles
Square: all sides equal + angles 90° + diagonals equal & perpendicular
Trapezium: only one pair of opposite sides parallel; median = 1/2(sum of parallel sides)
Kite: two pairs of adjacent equal sides; diagonals perpendicular; one diagonal bisects the other
Use coordinate geometry to find area, length of sides, midpoints, slopes, and diagonals
formulas: Area of trapezium = 1/2 × (sum of parallel sides) × height
examples: Find area of parallelogram with vertices A(0,0), B(4,0), C(5,3), D(1,3) using base × height
Length of diagonal of rectangle with vertices (0,0),(6,0),(6,4),(0,4) = √(6²+4²)=√52≈7.21 cm
Check if quadrilateral with coordinates A(0,0),B(2,2),C(5,2),D(3,0) is a parallelogram using slopes
Area of trapezium with parallel sides 6 cm & 10 cm and height 4 cm = 1/2*(6+10)*4=32 cm²
Diagonals of rhombus with d1=8 cm and d2=6 cm, area = 1/2*8*6 = 24 cm²
exam_tips: For coordinate geometry problems, always calculate slopes to check parallel/perpendicular sides
Use distance formula to find side lengths or diagonals
Label vertices properly and check orientation (clockwise/counterclockwise)
Apply Pythagoras theorem for diagonals if needed
For trapezium, identify parallel sides first before calculating median or area
